title: Bleaching wood with two-part wood bleach
subtitle: Use A-B oxalic wood bleach to remove stains, dark patches, and uneven tones from bare timber
excerpt: How to prepare bare timber by sanding and degreasing, apply part A and part B of a two-part oxalic wood bleach safely with correct neutralisation, sand back the raised grain, and assess the result before finishing.
sourceNotes: Furniture Restoration and Repair, William Cook. The Complete Manual of Woodworking, Jackson and Day. Historic England timber conservation guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A chemical bleaching system consisting of part A (sodium hydroxide solution) and part B (hydrogen peroxide solution), applied in sequence to bare timber. Part A is applied first and allowed to penetrate; part B is applied while part A is still wet. The oxidising reaction lightens the wood's natural pigments and removes stains. The bleached surface must then be neutralised with a dilute acid solution to stop the reaction and stabilise the pH before finishing.

The swelling and lifting of timber surface fibres caused by contact with water-based liquids including bleach solutions. After any water-based process, the wood grain rises and the surface feels rough. The raised grain must be sanded back with 240 grit paper before applying a finish — skipping this step leaves a rough surface under the finish.

A dilute acid wash applied to bleached timber to halt the bleaching reaction and restore the pH of the wood surface toward neutral before finishing. A standard neutralising wash is white vinegar diluted 1:4 with water (acetic acid). The wash is applied liberally, left for five minutes, then wiped off. Failure to neutralise can interfere with oil or lacquer adhesion.

content.text: Two-part wood bleach removes the natural tannin colour from timber and lightens dark water stains, ink marks, and uneven patches. The process is irreversible: the wood cannot be re-darkened to its original colour by the same method. The chemicals in the
A-B bleach
are corrosive. Work outside or in a ventilated space, and wear chemical-resistant gloves and eye protection throughout.
Step 1, protect yourself and prepare the surface

Put on nitrile or rubber gloves and close-fitting eye protection before opening either component. Remove the old finish completely: bleach does not penetrate lacquer, wax, or oil. Sand the bare timber to 180 grit with the sanding block, working with the grain. Use the card scraper to remove any glue squeeze-out near joints. Wipe the surface with a clean lint-free cloth to remove all dust.
Step 2, apply part A
Brush part A of the
two-part wood bleach

over the entire surface using a synthetic brush or a cotton cloth, working with the grain. Apply generously: part A must wet the surface fully. Do not allow it to pool in one area and dry unevenly. Leave it on the surface for the time specified on the product (typically three to five minutes).
Step 3, apply part B while part A is still wet

While part A is still wet on the surface, brush part B over the entire surface in the same direction. The reaction begins on contact: the surface will lighten visibly within a few minutes. Allow to dry fully, which typically takes two to four hours. The final colour after drying will be lighter than the wet colour. A second application can be made once the surface is dry if the result is insufficiently lightened.
Step 4, apply the neutralising wash
Once the surface is fully dry, apply the
neutralising wash

: white vinegar diluted 1:4 with water: over the whole surface with a cloth. Leave for five minutes, then wipe off with a damp cloth and allow to dry completely. The neutralising step is required before any finish is applied; bleach residue can prevent oil, wax, or lacquer from curing.
Step 5, sand back the raised grain and finish
Once dry, the surface will feel rough due to
grain raising

. Sand lightly with 240 grit paper on the sanding block, working with the grain, until the surface is smooth to the touch. Do not press hard: the bleached surface is ready for finishing and does not need re-shaping. Apply the chosen finish within 48 hours to prevent the grain from absorbing environmental moisture unevenly.
Variations

Oxalic acid solution is a single-component bleach. It removes iron tannate stains (the blue-black marks from iron fixings reacting with oak tannin) without lightening the surrounding wood. Use it for spot stain removal on oak and chestnut where the overall colour must stay the same. Two-part bleach lightens the whole surface and is used when the overall colour or a large uneven patch needs correcting.

attrs.symptom: The bleach appears to have no effect on the dark area
attrs.cause: Old finish not fully removed; bleach cannot penetrate lacquer, wax, or oil.
attrs.fix: Strip the finish completely with a finish remover appropriate to the original coating. Sand back to bare wood before re-applying the bleach.

attrs.symptom: Uneven pale patches appear on the surface after bleaching
attrs.cause: Part A applied unevenly; pooling in one area produced a stronger reaction than elsewhere.
attrs.fix: Allow to dry and apply a second full application of the two-part bleach to the whole surface, not just the uneven areas. A whole-surface second pass is more controllable than spot-treating.

attrs.symptom: The finish fails to adhere or remains tacky after bleaching
attrs.cause: Surface not neutralised; bleach residue raised the pH above the range the finish binder needs to cure.
attrs.fix: Strip the tacky finish. Apply the neutralising wash again, allow to dry fully, sand lightly with 240 grit, and re-apply the finish. Confirm the product is compatible with bleached timber — some water-based lacquers require a minimum cure time before applying over bleach.
